title: Core-First Language Theory (CFLT): A Discourse-Level Linearization Protocol for Cross-Linguistic Communication and LLM Prompting
description: Core-First Language Theory (CFLT) is a normative discourse-level linearization protocol that fixes the relative order of two constituent tiers as [Core] → [Reason] → [Space] → [Time]: the speaker's salience anchor (the event, identity, state, or request being committed to) occupies sequence-initial position, and the circumstantial ground frame of reason, place, and time follows. CFLT operates above the morphosyntactic level — each language assembles the event nucleus (the Core) in its native syntax, while the protocol governs only the order of the ground frame and the boundary between the two. It addresses a structural bottleneck shared by second-language acquisition and Large Language Model prompting: the cost of linearizing a multidimensional semantic representation into a single ordered string. Adult L2 learners pay it as working-memory overhead; LLMs pay it as order-sensitivity and Lost-in-the-Middle attention degradation. A pilot study (720 trials: 24 cases × 2 conditions × 3 runs × 5 frontier models, English + Mandarin) gives preliminary evidence on the LLM-prompting side — Core-first prompts raised extraction accuracy on a distractor-heavy condition from a mean of 65.6% to 100% across all five models and reduced completion tokens by up to 38% on reasoning models, with one model showing no benefit on a buried-decision variant; results are framed as suggestive, not confirmatory. The human-side predictions are reserved for a falsifiable research agenda. Reproducible data, prompts, and evaluation scripts are included in the repository.
notes: Version 2026-06 supersedes 2026-05b (tag zenodo-2026-05b). The abstract and introduction were reworked into a plain four-part lead (what / why / how / evidence) for readability; long specialist defenses were relocated to footnotes; promotional service mentions were removed from the prose while their disclosure remains in the released evaluation code; and an arithmetic slip in the abstract trial-count factorization was corrected (the total, 720 trials, was unchanged and always correct). No empirical results changed. The concept DOI 10.5281/zenodo.20289504 always resolves to the latest version.
